findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a solid, enhancing soft tissue mass centred within the cavernous sinus, which appears isointense to grey matter on both T1 and T2-weighted MRI sequences. The mass characteristically encases and severely narrows the cavernous segment of the internal carotid artery.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include intense, homogeneous contrast enhancement and the presence of a dural tail extending along the adjacent middle cranial fossa. There may also be associated hyperostosis of the adjacent sphenoid bone or anterior clinoid process.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of multiple prominent vascular flow voids or asymmetric dilation of the superior ophthalmic vein to suggest a carotid-cavernous fistula. Furthermore, there is no smooth expansile remodeling or erosion of the adjacent skull base typically seen with a schwannoma.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the intense, homogeneous enhancement of a cavernous sinus mass that completely encases and narrows the internal carotid artery.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Cavernous sinus meningioma, given the classic combination of an intensely enhancing mass with a dural tail that narrows the encased internal carotid artery. This is the most common benign tumor of the cavernous sinus and frequently presents with cranial nerve deficits such as ophthalmoplegia.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Meningioma, however as we have specified Cavernous sinus meningioma as the primary diagnosis, another major consideration is a Carotid-cavernous fistula (CCF). CCF, however, typically presents with prominent vascular flow voids, profound cavernous sinus distension, and a dilated superior ophthalmic vein secondary to arterial-to-venous shunting, rather than a solid soft tissue mass.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Schwannoma, given that trigeminal schwannomas frequently arise in or extend into the cavernous sinus, and missing this could drastically alter the patient's neurosurgical plan and neurological prognosis. However, schwannomas classically cause smooth bony remodeling or erosion of the skull base, are often T2 hyperintense, and notably displace rather than narrow the internal carotid artery.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the extent of the mass, specifically its relationship to the internal carotid artery, the optic chiasm, and its extension into adjacent compartments such as Meckel's cave or the orbit.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to establish the growth rate of this typically slow-growing tumor and to evaluate for any previously documented hyperostosis on old CT scans.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated high-resolution MRI of the skull base and cavernous sinus if only a routine brain scan has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the involvement of individual cranial nerves and the internal carotid artery.
management.ctPhases: If CT is performed, I would specify a thin-slice bone window algorithm to optimally assess for the pathognomonic hyperostosis of the adjacent sphenoid bone or skull base, which strongly supports a meningioma.
management.mriSequences: I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences with fat saturation to assess the dural tail and cavernous sinus margins, alongside T2-weighted sequences to differentiate it from the typically T2-hyperintense schwannoma and magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) to evaluate the patency of the encased internal carotid artery.
management.followUp: If the lesion is small and the patient is asymptomatic or a poor surgical candidate, I would suggest serial MRI surveillance at an interval of 6 to 12 months as per neuro-oncology guidelines to monitor for progression.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Skull Base/Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of the mass, the degree of narrowing of the internal carotid artery, and any mass effect on the optic chiasm to guide complex management decisions regarding surgical resection versus stereotactic radiosurgery.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a solid, enhancing soft tissue mass centred within the cavernous sinus, which appears isointense to grey matter on both T1 and T2-weighted MRI sequences. The mass characteristically encases and severely narrows the cavernous segment of the internal carotid artery. Supporting features include intense, homogeneous contrast enhancement and the presence of a dural tail extending along the adjacent middle cranial fossa. There may also be associated hyperostosis of the adjacent sphenoid bone or anterior clinoid process. Importantly, there is no evidence of multiple prominent vascular flow voids or asymmetric dilation of the superior ophthalmic vein to suggest a carotid-cavernous fistula. Furthermore, there is no smooth expansile remodeling or erosion of the adjacent skull base typically seen with a schwannoma. The feature that most influences my thinking is the intense, homogeneous enhancement of a cavernous sinus mass that completely encases and narrows the internal carotid artery.

The appearances are most in keeping with Cavernous sinus meningioma, given the classic combination of an intensely enhancing mass with a dural tail that narrows the encased internal carotid artery. This is the most common benign tumor of the cavernous sinus and frequently presents with cranial nerve deficits such as ophthalmoplegia. The principle differential would be Meningioma, however as we have specified Cavernous sinus meningioma as the primary diagnosis, another major consideration is a Carotid-cavernous fistula (CCF). CCF, however, typically presents with prominent vascular flow voids, profound cavernous sinus distension, and a dilated superior ophthalmic vein secondary to arterial-to-venous shunting, rather than a solid soft tissue mass. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Schwannoma, given that trigeminal schwannomas frequently arise in or extend into the cavernous sinus, and missing this could drastically alter the patient's neurosurgical plan and neurological prognosis. However, schwannomas classically cause smooth bony remodeling or erosion of the skull base, are often T2 hyperintense, and notably displace rather than narrow the internal carotid artery.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately define the extent of the mass, specifically its relationship to the internal carotid artery, the optic chiasm, and its extension into adjacent compartments such as Meckel's cave or the orbit. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is crucial to establish the growth rate of this typically slow-growing tumor and to evaluate for any previously documented hyperostosis on old CT scans. I would recommend a dedicated high-resolution MRI of the skull base and cavernous sinus if only a routine brain scan has been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the involvement of individual cranial nerves and the internal carotid artery. If CT is performed, I would specify a thin-slice bone window algorithm to optimally assess for the pathognomonic hyperostosis of the adjacent sphenoid bone or skull base, which strongly supports a meningioma. I would specify high-resolution T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences with fat saturation to assess the dural tail and cavernous sinus margins, alongside T2-weighted sequences to differentiate it from the typically T2-hyperintense schwannoma and magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) to evaluate the patency of the encased internal carotid artery. If the lesion is small and the patient is asymptomatic or a poor surgical candidate, I would suggest serial MRI surveillance at an interval of 6 to 12 months as per neuro-oncology guidelines to monitor for progression. This should go to the Skull Base/Neuro-oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact dimensions of the mass, the degree of narrowing of the internal carotid artery, and any mass effect on the optic chiasm to guide complex management decisions regarding surgical resection versus stereotactic radiosurgery.
